perturbation.original_text: Constellation shall, at its own expense, secure and maintain in full force and effect throughout the Term (a) insurance coverage for defamation, trademark and service mark infringement, unfair competition, copyright infringement, and infringement of a person's right of publicity and right of privacy from a carrier with an A.M. Best rating of A10 or better in an amount not less than [***] per occurrence; and (b) a general liability insurance policy from a carrier with an A.M. Best rating of A10 or better in an amount not less than [***] in aggregate. The liability limits may be met with any combination of primary and excess or umbrella insurance policy limits. Constellation shall provide the HOF Entities with certificates of insurance, naming each HOF Entity as an additional insured, evidencing the existence of such insurance policies within ten (10) days after execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Constellation shall secure and maintain insurance coverage for defamation, trademark and service mark infringement, unfair competition, copyright infringement, and infringement of a person's right of publicity and right of privacy and a general liability insurance policy. Constellation may provide the HOF Entities with certificates of insurance evidencing the existence of such insurance policies.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for specific insurance coverage amounts and carrier ratings, and the deadline for providing certificates. This creates a legal contradiction as Ohio law, where the agreement is to be governed, requires businesses to maintain adequate insurance coverage and to provide proof of such coverage upon request.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 3937.18 mandates certain minimum insurance standards and requires businesses to maintain adequate liability coverage, and to offer proof of coverage to affected parties. By removing the specific coverage amounts, A.M. Best rating requirements, and the ten-day deadline for providing certificates, the modified text contradicts this law. The secondary source references Ohio case law which describes the importance of insurance, and the requirements, under Ohio Law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 3937.18 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 3937.18 | Uninsured and underinsured motorist coverage. Ohio Revised Code / Title 39 Insurance / Chapter 3937 Casualty Insurance; Motor Vehicle Insurance Effective: March 14, 2023 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 56 - 134th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Any policy of insurance delivered or issued for delivery in this state with respect to any motor vehicle registered or principally garaged in this state that insures against loss resulting from liability imposed by law for bodily injury or death suffered by any person arising out of the ownership, maintenance, or use of a motor vehicle, may, but is not required to, include uninsured motorist coverage, underinsured motorist coverage, or both uninsured and underinsured motorist coverages. Unless otherwise defined in the policy or any endorsement to the policy, "motor vehicle," for purposes of the uninsured motorist coverage, underinsured motorist coverage, or both uninsured and underinsured motorist coverages, means a self-propelled vehicle designed for use and principally used on public roads, including an automobile, truck, semi-tractor, motorcycle, and bus. "Motor vehicle" also includes a motor home, provided the motor home is not stationary and is not being used as a temporary or permanent residence or office. "Motor vehicle" does not include a trolley, streetcar, trailer, railroad engine, railroad car, motorized bicycle, golf cart, off-road recreational vehicle, snowmobile, fork lift, aircraft, watercraft, construction equipment, farm tractor or other vehicle designed and principally used for agricultural purposes, mobile home, vehicle traveling on treads or rails, or any simil

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes critical requirements found in the original contract, specifically regarding insurance coverage amounts, carrier ratings, and deadlines for providing proof of insurance. The perturbation explanation states Ohio law requires businesses to maintain adequate insurance and offer proof upon request. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage (which seems tangentially related at best), the Law Explanation generated by the LLM states that Ohio Revised Code § 3937.18 mandates minimum insurance standards. By omitting these standards, the changed text undermines the legal obligation to maintain adequate coverage, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Ohio, without reference to principles of conflicts of law. Any suit or action filed or otherwise commenced in connection with this Agreement must be filed and litigated in an appropriate court located in the City of Canton, Ohio (provided, however, that if the suit or action involves a claim for which federal courts have exclusive jurisdiction, then such suit or action must be filed in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of the State of Ohio in Akron, Ohio), or such other venue as deemed appropriate by the HOF Entities. In the event of a dispute between an HOF Entity and Constellation regarding their rights and duties hereunder, the non-prevailing Party in any ensuing litigation shall pay the reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses of the prevailing Party (including costs of discovery and expert witness fees).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at the sole discretion of HOF Village, LLC, without reference to principles of conflicts of law. Any suit or action filed or otherwise commenced in connection with this Agreement must be filed and litigated in a court of HOF Village, LLC's choosing. In the event of a dispute between an HOF Entity and Constellation regarding their rights and duties hereunder, each party shall bear its own attorneys’ fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the specified jurisdiction (Ohio) and allows HOF Village, LLC, complete freedom in choosing the governing law and venue. It also removes the provision that the non-prevailing party pays legal fees. This creates a potential contradiction with Ohio Revised Code § 1345.03(B), which provides certain protections for consumers in transactions and implies a fair and reasonable venue for resolving disputes, in line with consumer protection measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1345.03(B) addresses unfair or deceptive acts or practices in consumer transactions. While this agreement isn't explicitly a consumer transaction, the broad venue and fee-shifting changes could be interpreted as circumventing the spirit of consumer protection by creating an unreasonable barrier for Constellation (potentially seen as the 'consumer' in this specific sponsorship context) to pursue claims under Ohio law, if applicable. The secondary site details specific protections for consumers afforded under Ohio Law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1345.03 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1345.03 | Unconscionable consumer sales acts or practices. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1345 Consumer Sales Practices Effective: April 6, 2017 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) No supplier shall commit an unconscionable act or practice in connection with a consumer transaction. Such an unconscionable act or practice by a supplier violates this section whether it occurs before, during, or after the transaction. (B) In determining whether an act or practice is unconscionable, the following circumstances shall be taken into consideration: (1) Whether the supplier has knowingly taken advantage of the inability of the consumer reasonably to protect the consumer's interests because of the consumer's physical or mental infirmities, ignorance, illiteracy, or inability to understand the language of an agreement; (2) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that the price was substantially in excess of the price at which similar property or services were readily obtainable in similar consumer transactions by like consumers; (3) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into of the inability of the consumer to receive a substantial benefit from the subject of the consumer transaction; (4) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that there was no reasonable probability of payment of the obligation in full by the consumer; (5) Whether the supplier required the consumer to enter into a consumer transaction on terms the supplier knew were substantially one-side
perturbation.justification: The modified contract contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1345.03(B) by allowing HOF Village, LLC to choose the governing law and venue and by removing the provision that the non-prevailing party pays legal fees. This creates a potential barrier for Constellation to pursue claims under Ohio law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 1345.03(A), states that 'No supplier shall commit an unconscionable act or practice in connection with a consumer transaction,' and Section 1345.03(B) requires consideration of circumstances to determine if an act is unconscionable, including whether the supplier has taken advantage of the consumer's inability to protect their interests. By granting HOF Village, LLC the sole discretion to choose the governing law and venue, the modified contract increases the potential for HOF Village, LLC to take advantage of Constellation's position, especially concerning legal fees, potentially rendering the agreement one-sided. This undermines consumer protection measures.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the HOF Entities or Constellation may terminate this Agreement at any time without liability if association with another Party could, in such Party's reasonable opinion, materially damage its reputation or image or in the event a Party breaches Section 3.3 hereof, which breach is not cured within sixty (60). days of receipt of notice of such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Either the HOF Entities or Constellation may terminate this Agreement at any time without liability, for any reason. Termination shall be effective immediately upon written notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows either party to terminate the agreement for any reason, without a cure period, removing the clause that speaks to reputational damage. This creates a potential conflict with Ohio contract law, which implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance. Ohio Revised Code § 1301.304 imposes an obligation of good faith. This modification allows arbitrary termination, which a court might see as violating that duty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1301.304 codifies the obligation of good faith in the performance or enforcement of every contract or duty. By allowing termination for 'any reason,' without regard to good faith or fair dealing, the modified clause could be interpreted as contradicting this implied duty. Although the UCC primarily governs the sale of goods, the principle of good faith is generally applicable to all contracts under Ohio common law. The secondary link is a resource that supports this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1301.304 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1301.304 | Obligation of good faith - UCC 1-304. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1301 General Provisions Effective: June 29, 2011 Latest Legislation: House Bill 9 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Obligation of good faith [UCC 1-304] Every contract or duty within Chapters 1301., 1302., 1303., 1304., 1305., 1307., 1308., 1309., and 1310. of the Revised Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Available Versions of this Section June 29, 2011 – House Bill 9 - 129th General Assembly [ View June 29, 2011 Version ]
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows either party to terminate the agreement 'for any reason,' removing the requirement for a cure period and any consideration of reputational damage. This directly contradicts the obligation of good faith in contract performance as mandated by Ohio Revised Code § 1301.304, which states that 'Every contract or duty within Chapters 1301., 1302., 1303., 1304., 1305., 1307., 1308., 1309., and 1310. of the Revised Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The scraped law snippet 2 similarly indicates that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Allowing termination for any reason undermines this duty, creating a contradiction.
